exemplar.title: To what extent does the ULEZ scheme effectively reduce the negative external costs and economic inefficiencies contributed by air pollution in London since its extension in 2020?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent does the ULEZ scheme effectively reduce the negative external costs and economic inefficiencies contributed by air pollution in London since its extension in 2020?

subject.title: Economics
subject.description:
**IB Economics** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **microeconomics, macroeconomics, the global economy, and development economics**, with a strong focus on **analytical and evaluative skills, real-world application of economic theory, and quantitative analysis**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Extended response questions | 1h 15min | 1h 30min | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Data response questions | 1h 45min | 1h 45min | 40% (SL), 30% (HL) |
| **Paper 3** | Mathematical questions | N/A | 1h | 20% (HL only) |
| **Internal Assessment** | Portfolio of 3 commentaries | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Research question is concise, focused and effectively connected to subsequent analysis.
Topic is accurately communicated and sustained throughout the essay.
Methodology section is clearly identified with a reasonable range of secondary sources and analytical tools.
weaknesses: Methodology lacks any primary data collection and relies on large simplifying assumptions.
Repetition of the research question in prose feels redundant in places.
Statistical or econometric methods are not fully specified, limiting the robustness of the approach.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates clear and accurate communication of topic and research question, meriting a high band performance in Focus and Method. However, the absence of primary data and only partially informed methodological choices (e.g., heroic cost allocations) prevent a top-band award. On balance, a score of 4/6 reflects strong focus but room for methodological improvement.

strengths: Confident grasp of welfare-economic theory, with accurate definitions and coherent framing.
Consistent and accurate use of economic terminology and notation throughout.
Integration of official statistics and academic literature to support theoretical arguments.
weaknesses: Application of some source material relies on dated or context-specific data (e.g., US estimates) that weaken relevance.
Occasional grammatical slips in policy context descriptions reduce overall clarity.
Figures and tables draw on bold assumptions without sensitivity or alternative scenarios.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay shows clear and coherent disciplinary knowledge (6/6) and very accurate use of terminology (6/6), but source application is only mostly appropriate (4/6). A score of 4/6 balances these strengths against the partial overreliance on simplified or dated data.

strengths: Analysis is detailed, with multiple diagrams, quantified welfare measures and clear consideration of short- vs long-run effects.
Balanced discussion that weighs effectiveness of ULEZ against equity and geographic concerns.
Acknowledgement of model limitations and data caveats demonstrates critical awareness.
weaknesses: Key cost allocations rest on rough assumptions without sensitivity analysis or robustness checks.
Critical evaluation of source bias is limited; cross-checking could be more systematic.
Discussion, while reasoned, stops short of deeper empirical validation or causality testing.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The studentâs critical thinking is solidly in the mid-band: research is relevant, analysis is clear and conclusions are generally supported, and evaluation is reasoned but not consistently deep. A mark of 5/12 fairly captures this performanceâstrong but with room for more penetrating critique and robustness.

strengths: Formal title page, table of contents and consistent section numbering align well with EE conventions.
Diagrams are labelled clearly, and bibliography and footnote style are uniformly applied.
Word count and page numbers are correctly stated.
weaknesses: Layout contains minor artefacts (placeholder brackets, OCR blemishes) that interrupt visual flow.
The table of contents is dense; grouping or simplification would improve navigation.
Inline lists occasionally disrupt narrative coherence and would benefit from fuller integration.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation is competent with only minor formatting and layout issues. While the structure is clearly appropriate, small distractions prevent a perfect score. A 3/4 reflects overall strong presentation with some opportunities to refine clarity and layout.

strandSelections.reasoning: The question â âTo what extent does the ULEZ scheme effectively reduce the negative external costs and economic inefficiencies â¦ since 2020?â â is concise, unambiguous and directly linked to the subsequent analysis of external costs, dead-weight loss and double-dividends.  Each section returns to the extent of effectiveness, demonstrating a tight connection between RQ and discussion.

The student states the topic in the title, repeats it in the first paragraph and explains why the ULEZ is of economic interest.  Five clearly-listed investigative angles show an explicit purpose and focus.  Throughout the essay the writing remains centred on measuring external costs, allocative efficiency and related outcomes, so the focus is maintained.  Hence topic communication is accurate and effective.

Source material is mostly relevant: official statistics underpin numerical estimates, journal articles support theory and news items illustrate equity concerns.  Figures and tables draw directly on these data.  Nevertheless, several figures rely on bold assumptions or dated US cost estimates, so application is âmostlyâ rather than âconsistentlyâ appropriate.

A formal title page, table of contents, logical headings and numbered sections mirror EE conventions.  Argument flows clearly from introduction to conclusion, satisfying the âclearly appropriateâ descriptor.

Word count, title and page numbers are stated; diagrams are labelled; footnotes and bibliography follow a consistent style.  Minor layout issues (placeholder brackets, OCR artefacts) are marginal, so overall formatting is correctly applied.

The student presents a balanced argument, weighing effectiveness against regressivity, optimal tax theory and geographic equity.  Limitations of data and models are acknowledged.  Evaluation is present but not always penetrating (e.g. no empirical test of causality).  This corresponds to a reasoned, partially critical evaluation.

Analysis is thorough: multiple diagrams, quantified welfare measures, and consideration of short-run versus long-run effects.  Conclusions are generally supported by calculations, although some steps (diesel XED) are sensitive to heroic assumptions.  Depth therefore fits securely in the 7-9 band, mid-to-high.

The essay shows confident grasp of welfare economics, Pigouvian taxation, Ramsey rule and double-dividend literature.  Definitions are accurate and theories are used correctly to frame arguments.  Occasional slips (e.g. âover-priced (P_F > P_S)â) do not materially detract from a clear, coherent display of disciplinary knowledge.

Most research cited is pertinent to the RQ and the student critically cross-checks government data with academic studies.  However, some figures (e.g. Â£3 bn external cost per vehicle) rest on rough allocations, so relevance is not âconsistentâ.  Thus quality aligns with the lower end of the 7-9 band.

A reasonable range of secondary sources (TFL data, Imperial College study, academic literature) is identified and the student justifies cross-checking to avoid bias.  Tools (diagrams, calculations) are named.  However, primary data collection is absent and some large simplifying assumptions (e.g. all Â£2.5 bn cost attributed to commuter cars) reveal only partially informed selection.  Hence methodology is âgenerally relevantâ rather than fully appropriate.

Economic terminology (MSC, dead-weight loss, dynamic efficiency, PED, XED) is used consistently and accurately.  The essay integrates symbolic notation correctly in formulas and diagrams, meeting the highest descriptor for this strand.

annotations.comment: Table of Contents header is well positioned and clearly indicates section structure.
The TOC table is comprehensive but very dense; consider simplifying or grouping subsections for quicker navigation.
Introduction section is clearly labelled, aiding the reader in understanding report flow.
The policy context is outlined but the vehicleâcharging mechanism description needs grammatical refinement for clarity.
The title is clearly stated and aligns with the investigationâs focus, meeting formal report requirements.
The research question is concise, focused and effectively connected to subsequent analysis.
A clear word count is provided and falls within the prescribed limit.
Superscript reference interrupts flow and currency notation is inconsistent; streamline formatting.
Application of Welfare Economics is accurate and demonstrates sound theoretical knowledge.
The inline list of vehicle standards is correct but flows awkwardly; integrate with narrative or use complete sentences.
The definition of negative consumption externalities is correct but would benefit from a concrete healthâcost example.
Listing five investigative angles provides clear focus and links directly to the research question.
Reiteration of the RQ in prose is somewhat redundant; tighten phrasing to avoid repetition.
Methodology section is clearly identified, outlining the studentâs research approach.
Acknowledges potential misinformation but omits primary data; consider interviews or surveys to enrich methodology.
Good crossâchecking of official data with academic sources, but add a critical evaluation of each sourceâs bias.
Discussion of diagrammatic tools is clear; further specify any statistical or econometric methods used.
Section header effectively signals discussion of external costs and theoretical framing.
The breakdown of perâvehicle external cost is logical, but include sensitivity checks for core assumptions.
Figure 1 is well labelled and clearly illustrates the negative consumption externality.
Model limitation is acknowledged, showing depth in critical evaluation.
Deadweight Loss section is clearly introduced, aiding structural navigation.
Ensure diagram axes are legible and scales explicitly annotated to improve reader comprehension.
Deadweight loss calculation is correct but millionâunit notation should be clarified.
